frame_analyses.description: Title slide: 'Tips for how to win the laparoscopic suturing contests' by Kengo Hayashi
Narration about importance of laparoscopic surgery and suturing contests in Japan

Text overlay: 'Rules of the contest in Japan' listing procedures: (I) inserting needle through trocar, (II) making a stitch, (III) suturing for 2 times (square knot), (IV) cutting thread and pulling needle out
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Educational slide with procedural rules. Note: mentions 'suturing for 2 times (square knot)' which differs from FLS standard (surgeon's knot + two single throws)

frame_analyses.description: Pink trainer box with concentric rectangular channels visible. Two needle drivers and black suture thread visible. Setup for demonstration.
Case with a long thread. Timestamp displayed: '19.8 sec'. Two needle drivers manipulating suture through the channels. Suture placement visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: First demonstration case showing longer thread scenario for speed comparison

frame_analyses.description: Case with a short thread. Timestamp displayed: '15.4 sec'. Two needle drivers visible with suture. Demonstrates shorter thread leading to faster completion.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Second demonstration case showing shorter thread scenario and time advantage

frame_analyses.description: Close-up view of needle drivers manipulating suture through trainer channels. Suture being placed through marked positions.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Technical demonstration of needle positioning and suture placement technique

frame_analyses.description: Suture looped and being manipulated by both needle drivers. Appears to show knot formation sequence.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Demonstration of knot tying mechanics with both instruments

frame_analyses.description: Knot appears to be cinched or tightened around the target area. Both needle drivers positioned near completed knot.

Text slide: 'Conclusion' with narrative: 'We compete the time not only for suturing, but for finishing all the procedures. I think you should focus on each part of the procedures, and notice which part of the procedure is your weak point.'
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Educational conclusion emphasizing holistic procedure timing and self-assessment

frame_analyses.description: Text slide: 'Summary of Tips' listing six key recommendations: (1) Adjust the length of a thread, (2) Grab a needle at the best position, (3) Image the position you are suturing, (4) Suture at 80% of the maximum speed, (5) Suture in the same stream, (6) Stick to the basics
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Educational summary of expert-level practical tips for suturing contests

frame_analyses.description: Final demonstration clip with timer visible at top: '00:00.03.95' showing real-time execution. Needle drivers actively manipulating suture through trainer.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final practical demonstration of speed and technique synthesis

confidence_rationale: Video classification is definitive. This is clearly an educational tutorial with narration, text overlays, title screens, and multiple demonstration segments. No actual individual performance is being scored.
cannot_determine: Actual completion time (not an individual performance)
Specific penalty instances (demonstrations only, not scored)
Knot quality assessment (multiple cases shown for teaching, not formal evaluation)
technique_summary: Expert-level instructional video demonstrating intracorporeal suturing technique for competition preparation. Content addresses procedural efficiency, tool manipulation, knot formation, and timing optimization.

demonstrated_techniques: Needle grip and positioning at optimal driver angle
Suture placement through marked entry/exit points on drain
Double-throw surgeon's knot formation with hand exchange
Thread length optimization for speed vs. control
Knot tightening and cinching under laparoscopic visualization
Rapid needle and suture management in confined workspace
verbal_cues: Focus on completion of all procedures, not just suturing speed
Adjust thread length strategically (short thread = faster completion time)
Grab needle at optimal position for best control
Image/visualize the anatomy before executing each stitch
Work at 80% maximum speed for control without hesitation
Master fundamentals before optimizing for speed
Identify weak points in your technique through deliberate practice
common_errors_addressed: Over-extending thread length leading to slow manipulation
Suboptimal needle grip causing awkward driving angles
Unprepared or unplanned needle trajectories causing hesitation
Excessive speed causing knot failure or misplacement
Departing from basic technique in pursuit of speed
teaching_content.context_notes: Video references Japanese laparoscopic suturing contest rules and procedures. Mentions 'square knot' or 'suturing for 2 times' which may reflect contest-specific variations from standard FLS protocol (which specifies surgeon's knot + two single throws with hand alternation). Demonstrates competitive context, not clinical FLS certification.

improvement_suggestions_not_applicable: This is instructional content, not a performance evaluation.
strengths_teaching: Clear breakdown of complex procedural sequence into manageable phases
Comparative demonstration of thread length impact on efficiency
Practical tips grounded in competition experience
Emphasis on balancing speed with precision and safety
Encouragement of self-assessment and weakness identification
